Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. At 29, I've got a lot going on outside of school, and studying kind of fits in wherever it can. I'd watch lecture videos when I had downtime, usually late at night when things quieted down. Sometimes I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then binge through materials when I felt guilty about falling behind. It wasn't organized—more like controlled chaos.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, heavily dependent on deadlines and whatever else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything. I'm juggling a lot at my age—responsibilities that younger students don't have to think about. When deadlines loomed, I'd cram. Otherwise, I drifted.
Q3: Usually it was fear, to be honest. I'd get that anxious feeling that I was missing something important or that an assignment was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were announcements or if I'd somehow missed a deadline. The peer support group would occasionally remind me to check in, which helped. But genuine curiosity about the material? That was rare.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got to a point where logging in felt automatic or natural. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do. The closest I got was during weeks when the peer support sessions were scheduled—those created some external accountability. But left to my own devices, I'd let days slip by without thinking about the LMS.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and watch things at my own pace, which worked better than reading dense text. I also found some of the diagrams and visual materials helpful for understanding concepts. The discussion board wasn't really my thing—I only posted once the whole semester.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums and most of the supplementary readings. They felt like extra work that wouldn't directly help my grade. I'd skim the readings at best, and I never really engaged with peer discussions beyond what was absolutely necessary. The optional practice quizzes? I kept telling myself I'd do them later, but later never came.
Q7: If I could see a direct line between the activity and passing the course, it felt worth my time. Anything that seemed like it would show up on an exam or count toward my grade got my attention. But if something felt like busywork or enrichment, I'd skip it. I know that's not the best attitude, but when you're stressed and scared about failing, you triage.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones created pressure and anxiety, but at least I knew I had to do them. Optional stuff felt like a trap—like if I started engaging with extras, I'd fall behind on the essentials. There was always this fear that I was already behind, so why add more to my plate?
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded most of the lecture slides and PDFs early on and studied from those on my tablet offline. I'd review them during commutes or when I didn't have internet access. I also used some YouTube videos to supplement topics I didn't understand from the course materials. None of that would show up in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours with printed notes or downloaded materials, working through problems by hand, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd watch a video once online, then rewatch the downloaded version multiple times. My actual study time was probably double what the system recorded, but it was invisible.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a video or PDF, watch or read it once online so it registered, then continue working with it offline for days. It was more convenient and didn't eat up my data when I was out. The LMS only saw that initial access, not the hours I spent with the material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was scared—genuinely scared—that I was going to fail. My stress levels were through the roof, and that fear became paralyzing rather than motivating. I'd log in, see how much I'd fallen behind, and feel overwhelmed. Sometimes I'd just close the browser and walk away.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big one—I'd come home exhausted and the last thing I wanted was to stare at more screens. The workload felt crushing at times, especially when multiple assignments overlapped. When I was confused about something, I'd sometimes avoid it entirely rather than ask for help. The peer support helped a bit, but my competing responsibilities always won.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. Just going through the motions without anything actually sinking in. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but at least I was technically 'engaged' according to the system.
Q15: All the time. I'd avoid assignments that seemed difficult or confusing, telling myself I'd figure it out later. The fear of doing it wrong made me procrastinate, which only made the fear worse. It's a vicious cycle—I knew the assignments were important, but anxiety made me avoid the very things causing the anxiety.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd watch my activity spike dramatically in the 48 hours before something was due. Without deadlines, I'd drift indefinitely. They created panic, but panic was apparently the only thing that could cut through my avoidance. My assignment completion rate reflects that—I missed too many.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but often I was too scared to look at it right away. I'd let feedback sit unread for days because I was afraid of what it might say. When I finally looked, if it was constructive, it helped me focus on what to fix. But the delay in checking it meant I couldn't always apply it to the next assignment in time.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having six online courses simultaneously meant I was constantly context-switching. The modular structure helped me see what needed to be done, but the sheer volume made it hard to prioritize. I needed more scaffolding, more check-ins, more structure to keep me accountable.
Q19: The visual materials and video content made engagement easier for someone like me. But the lack of mandatory participation requirements meant I could fade into the background too easily. Harder aspects were the dense reading materials and discussion requirements—I'm not a text-heavy learner, and forced discussions felt artificial.
Q20: That makes total sense to me. You can spend hours browsing, watching videos, reading forums, trying to understand something—but if you're confused or scared or overwhelmed, you might never actually complete the assignment. Activity doesn't equal output. Sometimes high activity is just anxious circling without landing anywhere productive.
Q21: That's somewhat me, actually. My exam score was okay, and I passed, but my LMS activity was sporadic. A lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials or external resources. The system doesn't capture everything. Some people are efficient—they get what they need quickly and don't need to linger online.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you stay. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They miss offline study, emotional state, whether you actually understood anything. They see the shadow of learning, not learning itself. A scared student frantically clicking around looks the same as an engaged one.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material and the course community. For me, I rarely felt that this semester. I was going through motions, trying to survive and pass. Real engagement would mean curiosity, investment, wanting to learn rather than just needing to not fail.
Q24: The fear. The constant, low-grade terror that I was going to fail, that I was too old to be doing this, that I'd wasted my time. The LMS can't see that I'd stare at the login page for ten minutes before working up the courage to check my grades. It can't see the relief when I passed, or how much the peer support actually meant to me emotionally, even if I only posted once.
